items.q: Does private/incognito browsing make you anonymous on the internet?
items.correct: No - it only stops the browser saving local history and cookies; websites, network providers and employers can still identify and track your activity.
items.misconception: Yes - incognito mode hides you completely from every website.
items.random_wrong: Yes - incognito mode encrypts everything you send.
items.why: The dark theme and the word 'private' oversell the feature. It is local-only privacy; your IP address and server-side logging are untouched.

items.q: If you recycle an old phone, is its environmental impact erased?
items.correct: No - most of a phone's footprint comes from manufacturing, mining and shipping, so recycling helps but extending the device's life helps far more.
items.misconception: Yes - recycling cancels out all the harm of making the device.
items.random_wrong: Yes - phones are made entirely of recycled material anyway.
items.why: Recycling is treated as an undo button. Lifecycle thinking shows production dominates impact - hence the advice to repair, reuse and only then recycle.

items.correct: Often not - many free apps are funded by collecting personal data and showing targeted advertising, so users pay with their data and attention.
items.misconception: Yes - a free app costs the company nothing and collects nothing.
items.random_wrong: Yes - free apps are charity donations from developers.
items.why: No price tag suggests no exchange. Reading data permissions reveals the actual business model, a key idea when discussing the impact of digital technology.

items.q: Which action most directly reduces the environmental impact of computing?
items.correct: Extending a device's life through repair and refurbishment
items.why: Correct answer: Extending a device's life through repair and refurbishment. “Replacing phones every year” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Personal data is best defined as:
items.correct: Any information relating to an identified or identifiable living person
items.random_wrong: Any file stored on a personal computer
items.why: It's Any information relating to an identified or identifiable living person. “Only data about celebrities” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Software, music and designs are examples of:
items.why: It's Intellectual property. “E-waste” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: An app that constantly tracks and shares your location mainly raises concerns about:
items.why: Correct answer: Privacy. “Screen resolution” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: A computer left on draws 200 watts. How many kilowatt-hours of energy does it use in 10 hours?
items.why: The real figure is 2 kWh. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
